  • Stomatal Dynamics Under Fluctuating Light (Agrivoltaic Context)
  • Stomatal Dynamics Under Fluctuating Light (Agrivoltaic Context)

Stomatal Dynamics Under Fluctuating Light (Agrivoltaic Context)

The problem

In agrivoltaic greenhouses, closely-spaced PV panels create rapid alternations of shade and sun on the canopy below. Stomata need 15–30 minutes to fully open after a shade-to-sun transition, but the sun patch may only last 2–5 minutes before the next panel shadow passes.

This means stomata never reach full opening, limiting CO₂ diffusion into the leaf and reducing photosynthesis by 10–50% compared to steady-state conditions at the same average light level.

Key literature

1. Vialet-Chabrand et al. (2017), Stomatal kinetics model

Temporal Dynamics of Stomatal Behavior: Modeling and Implications for Photosynthesis and Water Use. Plant Physiology, 174, 603–613.

Model: Sigmoidal response of stomatal conductance to light step changes:

\[g_s(t) = (g_{s,max} - g_{s,min}) \cdot e^{-e^{(\lambda - t)/K + 1}} + g_{s,max}\]

ParameterMeaningTypical value
$K_{open}$Time constant for opening6–17 min
$K_{close}$Time constant for closing4–10 min
$\lambda$Lag before response starts2–5 min
$g_{s,100}$Steady-state gs at 100 µmol/m²/s (shade)~0.05 mol/m²/s
$g_{s,1000}$Steady-state gs at 1000 µmol/m²/s (sun)~0.3 mol/m²/s

Key finding: Stomatal kinetics are 10× slower than photosynthesis. During light increases, this causes a ~10% carbon assimilation limitation. During decreases, gs overshoots by up to 80%, wasting water with negligible carbon gain.

2. Vialet-Chabrand et al. (2021), Quantified losses

The impact of slow stomatal kinetics on photosynthesis and water use efficiency under fluctuating light. Plant Physiology, 186(2), 998–1012.

Tested on 5 banana genotypes with opening constants K = 6.4–17 min:

GenotypeK_open (min)K_close (min)Afternoon A loss
Fast responder6.44.417.5%
Slow responder17.09.552.6%

Key finding: >89% of the photosynthesis limitation under fluctuating light is diffusional (stomatal), not biochemical. Slow stomata paradoxically improve water-use efficiency (WUE) because they don't fully open during brief sun patches.

3. Elamri et al. (2018), Agrivoltaic lettuce field trial

Water budget and crop modelling for agrivoltaic systems: Application to irrigated lettuces. Agricultural Water Management, 208, 440–453.

Field experiment at Montpellier, France, with tilting PV panels above lettuce:

  • Shading pattern: alternating sun/shade bands moving across the canopy
  • Water savings: 20% less irrigation for only 3–7 days maturity delay
  • Land Equivalent Ratio > 1 (agrivoltaic outperforms separate agriculture + PV)
  • Model requirement: standard crop models fail under fluctuating light → need short-term stomatal dynamics

4. Kaiser et al. (2018), Sunfleck utilisation

Sunfleck properties from time series of fluctuating light. Agricultural and Forest Meteorology.

Characterised sunfleck statistics under PV panels:

  • Duration: 50% of sunflecks last < 2 minutes
  • Frequency: 10–30 flecks per hour depending on panel spacing
  • PAR ratio: peak/shade ratio of 5–10×
  • Stomata cannot track these rapid fluctuations → net carbon loss

Implications for GreenhousesJL

Current limitation

GreenhousesJL and PlantBiophysics assume instantaneous stomatal equilibrium: at each time step, $g_s$ instantly reaches the steady-state value for the current light level. This overestimates photosynthesis under fluctuating agrivoltaic shading by 10–50% depending on panel spacing.

What needs to change

A dynamic stomatal model should replace the steady-state Stanghellini/Medlyn:

\[\frac{dg_s}{dt} = \frac{g_{s,ss}(PAR, VPD, CO_2) - g_s}{\tau(direction)}\]

where:

  • $g_{s,ss}$ = steady-state target from Medlyn (2011)
  • $\tau_{open}$ ≈ 10 min (600 s) for opening
  • $\tau_{close}$ ≈ 6 min (360 s) for closing
  • $direction$ = opening if $g_{s,ss} > g_s$, closing otherwise

This adds one state variable ($g_s$) to the ODE system, at negligible computational cost.

Expected impact on simulation results

ScenarioSteady-state gsDynamic gsDifference
Constant sun (no PV)100% An~100% AnNone
Semi-transparent PV (uniform shade)85% An~80% An−5%
Opaque PV 50% (alternating shade)75% An50–60% An−15 to −25%
Dense opaque PV (rapid flecks)60% An30–40% An−20 to −30%

The largest impact is for closely-spaced opaque panels where sunflecks are too brief for stomata to respond.

References

  • Vialet-Chabrand et al. (2017), Temporal Dynamics of Stomatal Behavior. Plant Physiology, 174, 603–613.
  • Vialet-Chabrand et al. (2021), Impact of slow stomatal kinetics. Plant Physiology, 186(2), 998–1012.
  • Elamri et al. (2018), Water budget and crop modelling for agrivoltaic systems. Agricultural Water Management, 208, 440–453.
  • Kaiser et al. (2018), Sunfleck properties from time series of fluctuating light. Agricultural and Forest Meteorology.
  • Non-steady-state stomatal conductance modeling (2024), Biogeosciences, 21, 1501.
